items.question: A pseudo-random number generator (PRNG) with the same seed will:
options: Produce the same sequence of numbers on reruns with the same software stack
Generate truly unpredictable hardware randomness
Guarantee identical GPU metrics across all machines
Eliminate the need to version data or code
items.answer: Produce the same sequence of numbers on reruns with the same software stack
items.explanation: PRNGs are deterministic algorithms; the seed sets their starting state. Environment and hardware can still cause drift.

items.question: For fair hyperparameter comparison, you should:
options: Change the train/val split seed for each trial
Fix the data split seed and vary only the hyperparameter under test
Never use seeds because they bias results
Use a different seed per epoch only
items.answer: Fix the data split seed and vary only the hyperparameter under test
items.explanation: Holding splits constant isolates hyperparameter effects from partition luck.

items.question: Which is NOT sufficient alone for full experiment reproducibility?
options: Logging random_state=42
Pinning library versions in a lock file
Recording git commit and data version
Setting only NumPy seed while PyTorch init stays unseeded
items.answer: Setting only NumPy seed while PyTorch init stays unseeded
items.explanation: Multiple RNG streams exist; partial seeding leaves hidden randomness. Logging seed 42 helps but still needs code, data, and environment alignment.

items.question: Environment pinning primarily addresses:
options: Which pseudo-random sequence is drawn
Which software versions and runtime execute the code
How many epochs to train
Which metric to optimize
items.answer: Which software versions and runtime execute the code
items.explanation: Pinning (requirements lock, Docker) stabilizes library behavior; seeds stabilize random draws inside that stack.

items.question: train_test_split(..., random_state=17) ensures:
options: The same train/test partition on reruns with the same sklearn version and data
Identical model weights after training
Deterministic GPU backward passes automatically
No need to log the split in experiment tracking
items.answer: The same train/test partition on reruns with the same sklearn version and data
items.explanation: random_state fixes the shuffle for the split; training randomness and environment still need separate control and logging.

items.question: A good reproducibility bundle to log with each run includes:
options: Seed, git commit, and environment identifier (lock file or Docker tag)
Final test accuracy only
The word 'reproducible' in the run name
Screenshots of loss curves
items.answer: Seed, git commit, and environment identifier (lock file or Docker tag)
items.explanation: These three let others approximate rerun conditions; metrics alone do not.
